patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
[PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
[PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the number of iterations in the shrink_delalloc function to prevent it from blocking indefinitely when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function now iterates over delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim all available space at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrarily long syscall latencies.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64
[PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64
[PATCH v4 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of `outstanding_extents` in the Btrfs inode structure from `u32` to `u64`. This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is limited by `LLONG_MAX`, which means that the maximum extent size computation can exceed the range of a 32-bit unsigned integer. The patch also updates the `mod_outstanding_extents` function to handle the new data type and adds some assertions to ensure that the inputs are within valid ranges.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v4 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where delayed reference reservations were under-estimated due to compression. When compression is enabled, the maximum extent size is reduced from 128MiB to 128KiB, leading to a significant discrepancy between estimated and actual extents. The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_inode_max_extents` to calculate the correct number of extents based on the inode's properties and the maximum extent size. This change ensures that reservations are accurately calculated even when compression is enabled, preventing under-reservation of delayed reference reservations for delalloc writes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v4 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem in the Btrfs file system where space is not reserved for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but this calculation does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic. The patch aims to fix this issue by reserving space for delayed refs during delalloc operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses stalls caused by sudden writeback on systems with large memory and disk sizes. The patches improve reservations for delayed refs in delalloc, reduce the size of work units in shrink_delalloc(), and make some basic smoothing changes to flush_space(). This is a targeted approach to mitigate issues related to metadata space system tailspins and user stalls waiting for ordered extents and delayed refs.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Simple quota unsafe? RIP: 0010:__btrfs_free_extent.isra.0+0xc41/0x1020 [btrfs] / do_free_extent_accounting:2999: errno=-2 No such entry
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a quota-related issue in the BTRFS file system, specifically an error that occurs when trying to free extents while running delayed refs. The problem arises from an invalid state caused by a previous transaction commit, leading to a '-2 No such entry' error. To fix this, the patch modifies the __btrfs_free_extent function to handle this specific scenario and prevent the quota-related errors. The approach taken involves adding additional checks and handling for the invalid state, ensuring that the BTRFS file system can recover from such situations without crashing or entering a readonly mode.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] userfaultfd: preserve write protection across UFFDIO_MOVE

version_history.subject: [PATCH] userfaultfd: preserve write protection across UFFDIO_MOVE

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch aims to preserve write protection across UFFDIO_MOVE by checking the uffd-wp condition of the source PTE before setting writable on the destination. This change is necessary because mremap() preserves the source write state unconditionally, but userfaultfd was not doing so. The fix involves adding two new conditions to check for uffd-wp and preserve it accordingly.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Clarified the userspace-visible impact of the bug and explained that it breaks backward compatibility. Agreed to come back with a new version.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun, Apr 12, 2026 at 11:18:07AM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Thu,  9 Apr 2026 11:28:22 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:
> 
> > move_present_ptes() unconditionally makes the destination PTE writable,
> > dropping uffd-wp write-protection from the source PTE.
> > 
> > The original intent was to follow mremap() behavior, but mremap()'s
> > move_ptes() preserves the source write state unconditionally.
> > 
> > Modify uffd to preserve the source write state and check the uffd-wp
> > condition of the source before setting writable on the destination.
> 
> Please can we have a description of the userspace-visible impact of the
> bug.
>

Simply:

  UFFDIO_MOVE silently drops write protection from the source PTE when
  moving pages to a destination, leading to missing write-protect faults
  after the page has been moved.

I ran into this while futzing around with some user space management of
VM memory, and expecting a move to continue firing WP faults after.

But Sashiko actually made a useful (though obtuse) observation which
has made me realize _MOVE is actually ambiguous on what to do with
source region UFFD modes.

> > +		if (pte_uffd_wp(orig_src_pte))
> > +			orig_dst_pte = pte_mkuffd_wp(orig_dst_pte);

This line assumes the destination must have intended to be WP, and the
the result is essentially stale uffd wp bits in the opposite case (a
user not intending to carry over WP now carries it over).

tl;dr: this is more of a semantic change than I'd intended, and the
existing tests did not catch it.

The correct solution here is to make a UFFDIO_MOVE_MODE_WP flag to copy
the UFFDIO_COPY_MODE_WP pattern.  Otherwise:

> (presently wondering if this is backward compatible)

Yes, you're right to wonder - this does break backward compatibilty.

Will come back around with UFFDIO_MOVE_MODE_WP.

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] Strategies for memory deallocation/movement for Dynamic Capacity Pooling

review_comments.author: Hannes Reinecke
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential use case for reshuffling memory behind a CXL switch to deallocate memory from one machine and reassign it to another. Suggested that the admin/orchestration needs better information on which memory blocks should be preferred for reassignment.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 4/13/26 23:10, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Mon, Apr 13, 2026 at 04:43:59PM +0100, Jonathan Cameron wrote:
>>>
>>> So quite some things to discuss; however, not sure if this isn't too
>>> much of an arcane topic which should rather be directed at places like
>>> LPC. But I'll let the PC decide.
>>
>> Superficially feels a bit arcane, particularly as we are currently
>> kicking untagged memory into the long grass as there are too many
>> open questions on how to present it at all (e.g. related to Gregory's
>> recent work on private nodes).  On recent CXL sync calls the proposal
>> has been to do tagged memory first and only support allocation of
>> all memory with a given tag in one go and full release.
>>
> 
> General consensus after last few months seems to be:
> 
> "While technically possible, untagged memory a bad idea for $REASONS"
> 
> I do not thing the private node case changes this, if anything it only
> changes where the capacity ends up.
> 
Thing is, there will be things like CXL switches. And with that we'll 
get CXL memory behind the switch, making it possible to reshuffle memory
'behind the back' of the application.
While the situation is similar to the current memory hotplug case
(and, in fact, the mechanism on the host side will be the same I guess),
the problem is now that we have a bit more flexibility.

The reason why one would want to reshuffle memory behind a CXL switch
is to deallocate memory from one machine to reassign it to another
machine. But as the request is just for 'memory' (not 'this particular
CXL card holding _that_ memory'), the admin gets to decide _which_
of the memory areas assigned to machine A should be moved to machine B.
But how?

And that basically is the question: Can we get the admin / orchestration
a better idea which of the memory blocks should be preferred for 
reassignment?
I'm sure there are applications which have a pretty flexible memory
allocation strategy which, with some prodding, they would be happy to 
relinquish. But I'm equally sure there are applications which react
extremely allergic to memory being pulled of underneath them.
And then there are 'modern' applications, which also don't like that
but for them it really doesn't matter as one can simply restart them.

So it would be cool if we could address this, as then the admin 
/orchastration can make a far better choice which memory area to
reassign.
And it might even help in other scenarios (VM ballooning?), too.

Cheers,

Hannes

I'm a little confused by how you imagine this memory actually gets used.

  1) Are you hotplugging directly into the buddy as a normal NUMA node
     and letting the kernel dole out allocations to anything?
     - i.e.: existing add_memory_driver_managed() interface

  2) Are you trying to plop the entire dynamically added extent into a

     exposed by a driver's /dev/fd ?

  3) Are you reserving this region specifically for in-kernel/driver
     use but not doled out to random users?

  4) Are you trying to just plop an entire extent into a VM (in which
     case you technically shouldn't even need to hotplug, in theory)?

  5) Are you trying to just decide which memory to release based on how
     much of it is used / hot / cold / etc?

I see lot of "Wondering if..." here based on what a switch COULD do, but
divorced from real use cases, 99.999% of what COULD be done is useless.

There are basically an infinite number of ways we should shuffle this
memory around - the actual question is what's useful?

Some use-case clarity here would be helpful.

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new mechanism to create private memory nodes, allowing devices to claim dedicated memory regions and manage their own memory allocation policies. The patch includes changes to the kernel's memory management subsystem, including modifications to the `for_each_node` function and the introduction of a new `__GFP_PRIVATE` flag for GFP_KERNEL allocations. This enables devices to allocate memory on specific nodes, improving performance in scenarios where network stacks require zero-copy operations.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that vhost-user requires access to all of a VM's memory, which cannot be achieved with MAP_PRIVATE and thus necessitates MAP_SHARED for most guest RAM.

Reviewer noted that disabling NUMA or reconfiguring it would break VMs, limiting the ability to run arbitrary guests, and requested consideration for this limitation.

Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out a misunderstanding of the fallback behavior for private memory nodes, noting that allocations targeting the special node should fall back to other nodes, but not vice versa.

Reviewer David pointed out that the patch does not provide a mechanism for fallbacks when allocating memory on private nodes, which would cause failures in certain scenarios such as alloc_shrinker_info() and slab allocations.

Reviewer David raised concerns about the private node's fallback logic, specifically the use of __GFP_THISNODE by kernel allocations, which could lead to unintended kernel allocations on the private node intended for user space. He suggested outright refusing non-user (movable) allocations targeting the node or discussing this in person.

Reviewer noted that guaranteeing memory stays within a private node only requires informing compaction and khugepaged to use __GFP_THISNODE, without needing __GFP_PRIVATE magic.

Reviewer David (Arm) questioned the necessity of making private memory nodes opt-in, citing concerns about why drivers cannot use these nodes with mbind and mempolicy
review_comments.raw_body:
I think with vhost-kernel virtio-net just supports MAP_PRIVATE, KSM and
all of that.

The problem is vhost-user, where the other process needs to access all
of VM's memory. That's not only a problem for virtio-net, but also
virtio-fs and all the other stuff that uses vhost-user.

One idea discussed in the past was to let vhost-user access selected
guest memory through QEMU, so there would be no need to even map all of
guest memory into the other processes.

That in turn would stop requiring MAP_SHARED for most guest RAM, only
focusing it on some key parts. Not sure what happened with that idea.

A related series proposed some  MEM_READ/WRITE backend requests [1]

[1] https://lists.nongnu.org/archive/html/qemu-devel/2024-09/msg02693.html


Something else people were discussing in the past was to physically
limit the area where virtio queues could be placed.

---

One problem might be that VMs with NUMA disabled or reconfigured would
just break. So you cannot run arbitrary guests in there. That was also
one of the problems of "physically limit the area where virtio queues
could be placed", if you have to be prepared to run arbitrary OSes in
your VM (Windows says hi).

---

But that's a different "fallback" problem, no?

You want allocations that target the "special node" to fallback to
*other* nodes, but not other allocations to fallback to *this special* node.

---

I assume you will be as LSF/MM? Would be good to discuss some of that in
person.

---

Needs a second thought regarding fallback logic I raised above.

What I think would have to be audited is the usage of __GFP_THISNODE by
kernel allocations, where we would not actually want to allocate from
this private node.

Maybe we could just outright refuse *any* non-user (movable) allocations
that target the node, even with __GFP_THISNODE.

Because, why would we want kernel allocations to even end up on a
private node that is supposed to only be consumed by user space? Or
which use cases are there where we would want to place kernel
allocations on there?

Needs a second thought, hoping we can discuss that in person.

---

Again, I am not sure about compaction and khugepaged. All we want to
guarantee is that our memory does not leave the private node.

That doesn't require any __GFP_PRIVATE magic, just en-lighting these
subsuystems that private nodes must use __GFP_THISNODE and must not leak
to other nodes.

---

I'm missing why these are even opt-in. What's the problem with allowing
mbind and mempolicy to use these nodes in some of your drivers?

I also have some questions about longterm pinnings, but that's better
discussed in person :)

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledges that the current RFC patch was informed by ZONE_DEVICE patterns, but ultimately chose a different design for this new interface. They agree that an ops struct is not the right design and plan to simplify the complexity of the patch series, which will address reviewer feedback about friction points.

The author is addressing David's (Arm) concern about the patch's compatibility and potential for other solutions. The author confirms that their approach works but acknowledges there may be alternative ways to achieve the desired result, specifically mentioning virtio's potential lack of interest in this feature.

Author acknowledges that fallbacks are needed in certain scenarios, agrees to re-examine the code and potentially revise it in a future version.

Author acknowledges a concern about making private nodes a permanent invariant and wonders if it would lock the design, but believes it could be changed without backward compatibility issues; no fix planned

The author is addressing concerns about whether all devices would support move_pages(), mbind(), and set_mempolicy() in the context of private memory nodes, acknowledging that not all devices may require these features but agreeing to give it serious thought.

Author responded to David's feedback on allowing mbind/mempolicy for compressed memory, explaining that it's not suitable due to the device's unique characteristics and potential dangers of write access. Author confirmed that these features are optional and mutually exclusive, but emphasized the need for transparent usage without requiring userland software to be less portable.

The author is addressing David's concern about the long-term pin extension being unnecessary for device memory, agreeing that it should be dropped as it doesn't make sense.

The author is addressing David's concern about how private memory nodes would handle slab allocations, specifically that fallbacks to other nodes are necessary for some allocations to succeed. The author suggests that the issue may not be in the folio-free path but rather in the task-death and VMA cleanup path.

Author acknowledges that hypervisor can make memfd with entire main memory region MAP_SHARED, and agrees to teach some services (migrate.c, mempolicy.c) how/when to inject __GFP_PRIVATE for a basic migration+mempolicy example.
review_comments.raw_body:
As a preface - the current RFC was informed by ZONE_DEVICE patterns.

I think that was useful as a way to find existing friction points - but
ultimately wrong for this new interface.

I don't thinks an ops struct here is the right design, and I think there
are only a few patterns that actually make sense for device memory using
nodes this way.

So there's going to be a *major* contraction in the complexity of this
patch series (hopefully I'll have something next week), and much of what
you point out below is already in-flight.

---

That is functionally what I did - the idea was pretty simple, just have
a separate memfd/node dedicated for the queues:

guest_memory = memfd(MAP_PRIVATE)
net_memory = memfd(MAP_SHARED)

And boom, you get what you want.

So yeah "It works" - but there's likely other ways to do this too, and
as you note re: compatibility, i'm not sure virtio actually wants this,
but it's a nice proof-of-concept for a network device on the host that
carries its own memory.

I'll try post my hack as an example with the next RFC version, as I
think it's informative.

---

This is fair, and I a re-visit is absolutely warranted.

Re-examining the quick audit from my last response suggests - I should
never have seen leakage in those cases, but the fallbacks are needed.

So yes, this all requires a second look (and a third, and a ninth).

I'm not married to __GFP_PRIVATE, but it has been reliable for me.

---

As a start, maybe? But as a permanent invariant?  I would wonder whether
the decision here would lock us into a design.

But then - this is all kernel internal, so i think it would be feasible
to change this out from under users without backward compatibility pain.

So far I have done my best to avoid changing any userland interfaces in
a way that would fundamentally change the contracts.  If anything
private-node other than just the node's `has_memory_private` attribute
leaks into userland, someone messed up.

So... I think that's reasonable.

---

This is where specific use-cases matter.

In the compressed memory example - the device doesn't care about memory
leaving - but it cares about memory arriving and *and being modified*.
(more on this in your next question)

So i'm not convinced *all possible devices* would always want to support
move_pages(), mbind(), and set_mempolicy().

But, I do want to give this serious thought, and I agree the absolute
minimal patch set could just be the fallback control mechanism and 
mm/ component filters/audit on __GFP_*.

---

First:

In my latest working branch these two flags have been folded into just
_OPS_MEMPOLICY and any other migration interaction is just handled by
filtering with the GFP flag. 


on always allowing mbind and mempolicy vs opt-in
---

A proper compressed memory solution should not allow mbind/mempolicy.

Compressed memory is different from normal memory - as the kernel can
percieves free memory (many unused struct page in the buddy) when the
device knows there's none left (the physical capacity is actually full).

Any form of write to a compressed memory device is essentially a
dangerous condition (OOMs = poison, not oom_kill()).

So you need two controls:  Allocation and (userland) Write protection
I implemented via:
    - Demotion-only (allocations only happen in reclaim path)
    - Write-protecting the entire node

(I fully accept that a write-protection extension here might be a bridge
 to far, but please stick with me for the sake of exploration).


There's a serious argument to limit these devices to using an mbind
pattern, but I wanted to make a full-on attempt to integrate this device
into the demotion path as a transparent tier (kinda like zswap).

I could not square write-protection with mempolicy, so i had to make
them both optional and mutually exclusive.

If you limit the device to mbind interactions, you do limit what can
crash - but this forces userland software to be less portable by design:

  - am i running on a system where this device is present?
  - is that device exposing its memory on a node?
  - which node?
  - what memory can i put on that node? (can you prevent a process from
    putting libc on that node?)
  - how much compression ratio is left on the device?
  - can i safety write to this virtual address?
  - should i write-protect compressed VMAs? Can i handle those faults?
  - many more

That sounds a lot like re-implementing a bunch of mm/ in userland, and
that's exactly where we were at with DAX.  We know this pattern failed.

I'm trying to very much avoid repeating these mistakes, and so I'm very
much trying to find a good path forward here that results in transparent
usage of this memory.

---

The longterm pin extention came from auditing existing zone_device
filters.  

tl;dr: informative mechanism - but it probably should be dropped,
it makes no sense (it's device memory, pinnings mean nothing?).

---

Yeah i'm trying to avoid it, and the answer may actually just exist in
the task-death and VMA cleanup path rather than the folio-free path.

---

inform the driver of a memory region with a task, the driver should have
a mechanism to take references on that VMA (or something like this) - so
that when the task dies the driver has a way to be notified of the VMA
being cleaned up.

This probably exists - I just haven't gotten there yet.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a bug in the sunrpc cache where requests with sequence number 0 were being silently skipped, causing netlink-based GET_REQS to return incorrect results and leading to NFS4ERR_DELAY hangs. The fix involves initializing the next sequence number at 1 instead of 0.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 8/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code

version_history.subject: [PATCH 8/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol: optimize stock usage for cgroup v2
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory usage for cgroup v2 by draining the stock of non-leaf cgroups when a leaf cgroup becomes its parent, reducing wasted percpu memory that will never be consumed unless all children are removed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memory control group (memcg) to use page_counter_stock instead of replicating its own stock handling logic. This change removes special casing for avoiding refilling and flushing of other memcgs' stocks, folds stock checking into the memory page counter, and allows for failing during page_counter_enable_stock() if there is not enough memory to allocate a percpu page_counter_stock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol: optimize memsw stock for cgroup v1
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory usage tracking in cgroup v1 by introducing a new stock for the memsw page counter, which is charged and uncharged independently from the memory page counter. This allows for better caching and reduces the worst-case scenario of having to walk two page_counter hierarchies. The change provides three possible scenarios: best case (both counters use cached stock), halfway scenario (one counter uses cached stock while the other doesn't), and worst case (neither counter uses cached stock). By introducing an independent stock for memsw, the patch aims to avoid the worst-case scenario more often and allow for separate failure or success of memory page counter and memsw page counter.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8 RFC] mm/page_counter: introduce stock drain APIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new functions, page_counter_drain_stock() and page_counter_drain_cpu(), to replace the existing memcg stock draining functions. The new functions are designed to handle rare operations such as draining system memory under pressure or when a cgroup is dying. They use work_on_cpu() to synchronously drain each online CPU's stock and synchronize with concurrent charge/uncharge via local_lock. page_counter_drain_cpu() handles the CPU hotplug dead path, where the stock can be accessed directly without locking since the CPU is dead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8 RFC] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_uncharge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page_counter_uncharge function to take into account the stock-awareness of page counters. It first checks if depositing the charge to the stock would put it over the batch limit, and if not, it deposits the charge immediately. If the stock is exceeded, it walks up the page counter hierarchy and uncharges the excess. This change preserves the existing semantics of the stock handling logic in try_charge_memcg. The repeated work of hierarchically cancelling page_counter charges is also extracted into a helper function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8 RFC] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_try_charge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page_counter_try_charge function to take into account the stock of a page counter when attempting to charge pages. It first checks if there is enough stock available, and if so, charges from the stock without hierarchical charging. If not enough stock is available, it attempts to fulfill the charge request hierarchically while also filling up the stock. If this fails, it retries with the exact number of pages requested. Finally, if the greedy attempt succeeds, it tries to add any surplus pages to the stock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8 RFC] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock to the Linux kernel's memory management system, allowing each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages and reducing the need for expensive hierarchy walks on every memcg charge and limit check. This addresses two main issues with the current implementation: the random eviction of memcgs when more than 7 are being charged, and the tight coupling between stock and memcg, which prevents new page counters from using separate stock caches. The patch adds a generic per-cpu stock to the struct page_counter and provides functions for enabling and disabling stock on a per-page_counter basis.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 0/8 RFC] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, addressing several limitations in the current design. The current implementation has a limit of 7 memcgroups per CPU, leading to random evictions and unnecessary hierarchy walks. By moving the stock to page_counter, the patch consolidates stock limit checking with page_counter limit checking, eliminates the need for css references and traversal overhead, and makes independent stock management scalable for future users.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the current implementation of per-cpu stocks in mem_cgroup, specifically the random eviction problem and tight coupling between stock and memcg. The author proposes moving the concept of stock down to the page_counter level, which would eliminate the 7 slot limit and make independent stock management scalable for future users. They introduce a generic per-cpu stock directly in struct page_counter and provide functions to enable, disable, and free it, but do not use these yet.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the patch's handling of stock management in page_counter_try_charge(). They explained that they preserved the same semantics as the existing stock handling logic, but made it stock-aware by adding a check against the stock before attempting a hierarchical charge. The author stated that the page_counter_stock is currently unused and no functional changes are intended.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the page_counter_uncharge() function not being stock-aware, which was pointed out by reviewer feedback. The author has modified the patch to make page_counter_uncharge() stock-aware and added a helper function to extract repeated work of hierarchically cancelling page_counter charges. As of this patch, the page_counter_stock is unused as it has not been enabled on any memcg yet.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the draining of per-cpu stock, introducing new functions page_counter_drain_stock() and page_counter_drain_cpu() to replace memcg stock draining functions. The new functions use work_on_cpu() for synchronous remote CPU drain and synchronize with concurrent charge/uncharge via local_lock. A fix is planned.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about the impact of moving stock management from mem_cgroup to page_counter, specifically that refilling the new stock would still cause unnecessary work by evicting other memcgs' stocks. The author explained that this is no longer a concern since the new stock does not randomly evict other memcgs' stocks, and instead only adds extra pages to the local cache. Additionally, the author noted that the old special-casing for !allow_spinning check has been removed, which previously avoided refilling and flushing of the old stock.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the lack of a fast path for cgroup v1's memsw page_counter after folding the memcg stock into the page_counter level, introduced an independent stock for memsw to avoid hierarchical charges and provide better caching.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about removing the memcg_stock logic, acknowledging that obj_stock is untouched and preserved for future use. They confirm that all code related to handling memcg_stock can be removed.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about unused stock in non-leaf cgroups in cgroup v2, explaining that it's wasted percpu memory and proposing to drain the stock when leaf cgroups become parents. They acknowledged a rare race condition but chose not to add synchronization mechanisms, instead accepting the pages kept captive by parent page_counters.
review_comments.raw_body:
In cgroup v2, tasks can only belong to leaf cgroups, meaning non-leaf
cgroups never receive direct charges. Having stock remain in these
cgroups therefore, is wasted percpu memory that will never be consumed
unless all of its children are removed.

To avoid leaving unused but accounted charges from remaining in non-leaf
cgroups, drain the stock when leaf cgroups become parents.

There is one caveat, which is concurrent charging and child creation.
When a leaf cgroup becomes a parent at the same time it is still
charging a task, there can be a race condition where the parent's
stock is drained, then refilled by the charge.

Instead of adding expensive synchronization mechanisms, accept the
pages kept captive by parent page_counters which will not be able to use
the stock until all its children are offlined first. It is a rare
race condition, and is also bounded by MEMCG_CHARGE_BATCH = 64 pages.

This optimization is not for cgroup v1, where tasks can be attached to
any cgroup in the hierarchy, meaning stock can be consumed & refilled
for non-leaf cgroups as well.

Suggested-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
---
 mm/memcontrol.c | 11 +++++++++++
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
index 6d50f5d667434..4be1638dde180 100644
--- a/mm/memcontrol.c
+++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
@@ -4130,6 +4130,17 @@ static int mem_cgroup_css_online(struct cgroup_subsys_state *css)
 	 */
 	xa_store(&mem_cgroup_private_ids, memcg->id.id, memcg, GFP_KERNEL);
 
+	/*
+	 * On v2, non-leaf memcgs cannot directly be charged. This child's
+	 * parent is no longer a leaf, so drain the parent's stock.
+	 */
+	if (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys)) {
+		struct mem_cgroup *parent = parent_mem_cgroup(memcg);
+
+		if (parent)
+			page_counter_drain_stock(&parent->memory);
+	}
+
 	return 0;
 free_objcg:
 	for_each_node(nid) {
-- 
2.52.0

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed a concern about the potential performance impact of moving stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, explaining that performance testing across various scenarios showed negligible differences and that the change was made to simplify draining.

The author is addressing concerns about overcharging in the worst-case scenario, where all CPUs cache idle stock. They acknowledge that this scenario is rare and difficult to reach, but agree to add a note about it in the next series revision. The author also mentions a potential user interpretability issue with unused charge being left behind without triggering a drain_all_stock.

The author acknowledges that working with memcg granularity was uncomfortable when trying to introduce a new page counter, specifically for enforcing strict limits. They mention this as motivation for moving the per-cpu stock down into the page_counter, but did not explicitly note it in the cover letter.

The author acknowledges that the current memcg slot scanning and traversal can be confusing, but doesn't consider it a major issue; they mention potential benefits of their proposed changes, specifically direct attribution of stock to the page cache and elimination of random evictions.

Author acknowledged that the patch's benefits are not primarily in performance gains, but rather in more interpretable semantics for stock management and transparent charging. They highlighted that systems using less than 7 cgroups will have reduced memory overhead and comparable performance, while systems using more than 7 leaf cgroups will benefit from reduced unnecessary hierarchy walks.

Author acknowledges a concern about performance testing and promises to add wall time perspective in the next version to provide more tangible results.

Author acknowledged that the cached charge mechanism may not be suitable for all scenarios and offered to revisit the issue, but did not commit to making changes.
sentiment_signals: offered to think more about it
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 13 Apr 2026 09:23:38 +0200 Michal Hocko <mhocko@suse.com> wrote:

Hello Michal,

Thank you for your review as always!

---

This is a great point. I would like to note though, that for systems running
less than 7 leaf cgroups (I'm not sure what systems typically look like outside
of Meta, so I cannot say whether this is likely or not!) this change would
be an optimization since we allocate only for the leaf cgroups we need ; -)

But let's do the math for the worst-case scenario:
Because we initialize the stock to be 0 and only refill on a charge / 
uncharge, the worst-case scenario involves a workload that charges
to all CPUs just once, so that it is not enough to benefit from the
cacheing. On a very large system, say 300 CPUs, with 4k pages, that's
300 * 64 * 4kb = 75 mb of overcharging per leaf-cgroup.

This is definitely a serious amount of overcharging. With that said, I
would like to note that this seems like quite a rare scenario; what
would cause a workload to jump across 300 CPUs? For this to be a regression
it also has to be 8+ workloads all jumping around the CPUs and storing
not-to-be-used cache on all of them, and anything below that would still be
an optimization over the current setup.

Also, let's talk about what happens when we do reach the worst-case scenario.
Once we reach the degenerate state where the stock is charged and the workload
has no intention of running on the CPUs with idle cache, we would eventually
reach the failure branch of try_charge_memcg, which drains all stock!

So IMO, I think the issue of overcharging isn't too bad. It's very difficult
to reach the scenario where all CPUs are caching idle stock, and the existing
recovery mechanism in try_charge_memcg puts us right back into the optimal
scenario where none of the CPUs have stock, and we only refill those that
the workload runs on. I'll be sure to add this in the next spin of the series,
since I think it's important to note (the other overhead being the memory
that we have to allocate percpu for each of the stock structs, which is
only 2 words/cpu/memcg (including parents). But still worth noting explicitly!)

Above is the perspective from the system, in terms of memory pressure and
overcharging. From a user interpretability POV, I think there is a gap between
when a workload litters unused charge everywhere, but there is not enough
memory pressure to trigger a drain_all_stock, so a user might be confused
why their workload is using so much memory.

I think this could be a problem. Especially if there is a userspace
load balancer that schedules work based on how much memory the workload is
using. At Meta we use Senpai in userspace to create benevolent memory pressure
that should be enough to reap cold memory (and also idle stock), but I'm
wondering what this will mean for systems that don't have such cold memory
purging mechanisms. I'll think about this a little bit more.

---

Yes of course. So to give some context, I realized that stock was a bit
uncomfortable to work with at a memcg granularity when I tried to introduce
a new page counter for toptier memory tracking (in order to enforce strict
limits. I didn't explicitly note this in the cover letter because I thought
that there was a lot of good motivation aside from the specific use case
I was thinking of, so decided to leave it out. What do you think? : -)

I'm not a memcgv1 user so I cannot tell from experience whether this is a
pain point or not, but I also did find it awkward that one stock gated the
charges for two page_counters memsw and memory, which made the slowpath
incur double the hierarchy walks on a single stock failing, instead of keeping
them separate so that it is less likely for both the page hierarchy walks
to happen on a single charge attempt.

---

I don't think this is really that big of a problem, but just something that
I wanted to note as a benefit of these changes. I remember being a bit
confused by the memcg slot scanning & traversal when reading the stock
code, personally I think being able to directly be able to attribute stock
to the page_cache it comes from, as well as not randomly evicting stock
could be helpful.

---

I think that the biggest strength for this series is actually not with
performance gains but rather with more interpretable semantics for stock
management and transparent charging in try_charge_memcg.

But to break it down, any systems using less than 7 cgroups will get
reduced memory overhead (from the percpu structs) and comparable performance.
Any systems using more than 7 leaf cgroups will benefit because stock is
no longer randomly evicted and needed to refill.

---

wall time perspective. But I can trace for how often we refill the stock
in the next version, and I hope that it can show more tangible results.

---

Thank you for the words of encouragement Michal!!!

On the point of cached charge, I hope that I've explained it above, I'll
think some more about that scenario as well. 

One last thing to note, that is orthogonal to our conversation here. Above,
I assumed 4k pages. But on systems with bigger base page sizes like 64k,
maybe it makes sense to lower the amount of stock that is cached. 
64 * 64kb = 4mb per CPU, maybe this is a bit overkill? ; -)

Thanks a lot for your thoughtful review, it is always appreciated.
I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
[PATCH v3] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new unprivileged ioctl, BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without exposing sensitive information about inaccessible files. The ioctl takes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_args with offset and length details, and writes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry into a provided buffer along with the checksums if available. This is primarily used to speed up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir by avoiding recalculating checksums for files that already have them stored on disk.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry::type should be bit flags instead of a single value, allowing for easier expansion in the future to return csums for encrypted or compressed data.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looks good to me for the current usage.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>


Although the discussion about encryption makes me wonder if 
btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry::type should be bit flags.

For now it's fine, but as Daniel mentioned, in the future we may want to 
return the csum of such encrypted/compressed data.
In that case we need extra checks on the @flags to determine if 
ENCRYPTED/COMPRESSED is followed by a csum or not.

If @type is bit flags, we can easily have things like 
COMPRESSED|HAS_CSUM to indicate that future change.

Feel free to ignore this, it's just making the future expansion easier, 
not critical and we can definitely find a way using the new @flags to 
handle future change.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the new ioctl is similar to an existing proposed ioctl for deduplication and suggested it could be a wrapper for the search tree ioctl on the checksum tree, rather than a standalone implementation.

Reviewer noted that repeated mkfs operations are not typical, but acknowledged the significant time savings for preparing images; also mentioned potential use by deduplication tools and required security pass on final version

Reviewer David Sterba suggested clearing the partial range globally at the start of the ioctl, regardless of how many data is returned, but noted that this could have a performance hit for large buffers and repeated calls.

Reviewer David Sterba noted that the ioctl is not interruptible, which would cause issues if the operation is interrupted while holding the inode lock; he suggested adding an inode lock helper or calling down_rwsem_interruptible() to address this.

Reviewer David Sterba requested initialization of a buffer with zeros using = { 0 } instead of relying on memset() for partially filled buffers, and also suggested checking the final patch for unnecessary line breaks that exceed 80 characters.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the past reading the checksums was desired for deduplication, and we
had some proposed ioctls but I think it was not extensive to also cover
the holes, compression. Basically a wrapper for the search tree ioctl on
the checksum tree.

Handling all the types in this patch seems to be complete.

---

Repeated mkfs is a specific use case, normaly it happens just once but I
understand that for preparing various images the time savings are
significiant.

Once this ioctl is available I think the deduplication tools will make
use of it as an initial filter before actually doing the real
deduplication of extents.

As this is a unprivileged ioctl and touching structures and memory we'll
need a security pass on the final version. So far I haven't see any
issues known to me.

---

Clearning the partial range here is good but I think it should be done
globally right at the start of the ioctl, regardless of how many data we
return.

OTOH this can have a peformance hit in case a large buffer is provided
by default but for various ranges and possibly short ones, not matching
the entire buffer. So repeated calls will have to clear 16M but return
a few kilobytes.

---

This needs to be interruptible, all other points are. There's no inode
lock helper for that so you'd have to add it or call
down_rwsem_interruptible().

---

Please initialize that right away with = { 0 }, it's done later by
memset() but for something filled partially it's better to do it at the
beginning than forgetting to do it later.

Overall comment, please check the final patch that there are not
unnecessary line breaks that overflow 80 chars only slightly. I've seen
a few and would have fixed it but I think I've been pointing that out
enough in the past. I'll fix anything that's left if needed.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the ioctl implementation needs to handle compressed extents correctly and agreed to address this in a future version.

The author addressed a concern about the likelihood of collisions in the deduplication logic, explaining that due to the birthday paradox, a 50% chance of collision occurs every 256 MB for 32-bit hashes and is negligible for larger hash sizes.

Author acknowledged that the ioctl interface is not set in stone and can be changed if needed, but emphasized its current speed benefits over manual calculation.
sentiment_signals: agreed to address in a future version
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks David.

On 14/04/2026 3.07 am, David Sterba wrote:

---

IIRC the mathematics of the birthday paradox is that for an n-bit hash, 
you need 2^(n/2) sectors to have a 50% chance of a collision.

For the 32-bit hashes, that means a 50% chance of a collision every 256 
MB. For the 256-bit hashes, that's 2^140 bytes... whatever that works 
out as. So the dedupe logic would no doubt be that for larger hash sizes 
GET_CSUMS itself will be sufficient.

---

That's fine - this isn't an interface change, so we can always change it 
later if it turns out to be a bottleneck. At any rate it's going to be 
faster than userspace calculating the csums manually.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds an ioctl (input/output control) command to the Btrfs file system called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows users to retrieve checksums of files. The main reason for this is to prevent potential security vulnerabilities by not exposing arbitrary checksums to unprivileged users. However, it also provides a useful feature for userspace applications that can XOR multiple checksums together for ad-hoc checksum verification.

review_comments.author: Daniel Vacek
review_comments.summary: reviewer argued that csums can be returned for compressed and encrypted extents, even if the permission check fails, by flagging them as ENCODED
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd say there is no reason not to return the csums. Even for
compressed or encrypted extents, you can flag them ENCODED and still
return the csum (given the permission check). Userspace is free to
ignore it.

Note that you can read/write the raw data with the
BTRFS_IOC_ENCODED_{READ|WRITE} ioctl.

Other than that it looks like a very nice speed-up.

--nX

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged Daniel's feedback that userspace might not want a copy of csums, agreed to remove this feature for now and consider adding it as a later flag.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks Daniel. My concern there is that this probably won't be what 
userspace wants, so you'll be doing a copy for no reason. I'll think 
I'll just keep it for a later flag, so we can add this later if and when 
we need it.

One thing I'm working on at the moment is "encoded reflinks", so you can 
mark uncompressed data as being part of a compressed extent or vice 
versa. If we extended this to encryption, this would be a use case for 
this ioctl: you would be able to do mkfs.btrfs --rootdir --reflink on an 
encrypted directory without needing to have loaded the key.

review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 13, 2026 at 8:29 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Wed, Apr 1, 2026 at 11:38 PM Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com> wrote:
> >
> > test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink sets up two cgroups: wb_group, which is
> > expected to trigger zswap writeback, and a control group (renamed to
> > zw_group), which should only have pages sitting in zswap without any
> > writeback.
> >
> > There are two problems with the current test:
> >
> > 1) The data patterns are reversed. wb_group uses allocate_bytes(), which
> >    writes only a single byte per page — trivially compressible,
> >    especially by zstd — so compressed pages fit within zswap.max and
> >    writeback is never triggered. Meanwhile, the control group uses
> >    getrandom() to produce hard-to-compress data, but it is the group
> >    that does *not* need writeback.
> >
> > 2) The test uses fixed sizes (10K zswap.max, 10MB allocation) that are
> >    too small on systems with large PAGE_SIZE (e.g. 64K), failing to
> >    build enough memory pressure to trigger writeback reliably.
> >
> > Fix both issues by:
> >   - Swapping the data patterns: fill wb_group pages with partially
> >     random data (getrandom for page_size/4 bytes) to resist compression
> >     and trigger writeback, and fill zw_group pages with simple repeated
> >     data to stay compressed in zswap.
> >   - Making all size parameters PAGE_SIZE-aware: set allocation size to
> >     PAGE_SIZE * 1024, memory.zswap.max to PAGE_SIZE, and memory.max to
> >     allocation_size / 2 for both cgroups.
> >   - Allocating memory inline instead of via cg_run() so the pages
> >     remain resident throughout the test.
> >
> > === Error Log ===
> >  # getconf PAGESIZE
> >  65536
> >
> >  # ./test_zswap
> >  TAP version 13
> >  ...
> >  ok 5 test_zswap_writeback_disabled
> >  ok 6 # SKIP test_no_kmem_bypass
> >  not ok 7 test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink
>
> I assume the test passed after fix? ;)

That's right! This patchset is targeted to thoroughly fix various bugs.
I rerun it too many times on various arches. All works well :).
